mappings.episodeTitle: The Zulu Nation's Rise and Fall
mappings.rationaleAI: Shaka's transformation of the Zulu into a formidable empire directly addresses C.4 (rise of the Zulu Empire under Shaka and 19th-century African migrations), while confrontations with Afrikaner settlers and the British, the Battle of Isandhlwana, and the kingdom's absorption into Natal cover C.6 (Cape eastern frontier conflicts), C.6.b (British colony of Natal), and C.7 (Zulu wars).

The 1884-1885 Berlin Conference established rules for European colonial claims that directly shaped the partition of southern Africa at the terminal date of this section, accelerating the imposition of arbitrary borders and colonial frameworks across the region during the era of mineral discoveries and confederation (C.7).

The VOC established the Dutch settlement at the Cape of Good Hope in 1652 (C.3), which became the foundation of European colonization in southern Africa; the company's displacement of Portuguese trading posts and its broader commercial and territorial ambitions provide essential context for the Cape Colony's origins.

British imperial expansion encompassing military conquest, settler colonialism, and colonial governance structures directly shaped southern Africa through the establishment of Cape Colony and Natal (C.6.b), conflicts with Boers and Xhosa on the Cape frontier (C.6), and the era of mineral discoveries that drew intensified British imperial interest (C.7).
